MOTION FOR CHANGE OF VENUE
Off Calendar Feb-06-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Long Do, Esq. 415-680-7419 of Athene Law LLP for Petitioner.
Carolyn Tsai, Deputy Attorney General, 415-703-5590 of the Office of the Attorney General. (Defendant's Counsel appears late after the case has been called and Orders signed.)

9:30 am. RULING - The matter is Uncontested. The Court adopted the tentative ruling.
At 10:47 am, the matter is recalled. The Court announces the tentative ruling and says it has signed orders. Defendant's Counsel appeared late and argues parties had a stipulation to continue the matters. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) After argument, the Court adopts the tentative ruling.

RESPONDENT CALIFORNIA DEPARTMENT OF HEALTH CARE SERVICES, RICHARD FIGUEROA's MOTION TO CHANGE VENUE.

Respondents' motion to change venue is denied.

Respondents' motion for mandatory transfer under Code of Civ. Proc. 393(b) is denied as waived. Code of Civ. Proc. 396b provides that a defendant must file a motion to transfer venue "at the time he or she answers, demurs, or moves to strike, or . . . without answering, demurring, or moving to strike and within the time otherwise allowed to respond to the complaint?." Section 396b "represents an explicit codification of the general 'waivability' of venue defects, providing that, notwithstanding the governing venue provisions, an action may generally be tried 'in the court where commenced' unless the defendant makes a timely motion for change of venue . . . ." (Barquis v. Merchants Collection Assn. (1972) 7 Cal.3d 94, 115.) Here, Respondents not only answered the petition on August 29, 2019 without moving to change venue, they expressly admitted the allegation in the petition that venue is proper in this county, as well as lodging the extensive administrative record. (Pet. 7; Ans. 7.) Thus, Respondents waived any objection to venue. (See Newman v. County of Sonoma (1961) 56 Cal.2d 625, 627-628 [provisions of Code of Civ. Proc. 394(a), permitting motion for change of venue to neutral county in action by or against city, county, or local agency, are not jurisdictional and may be waived by failure to bring timely motion].)

Respondents' alternative motion for discretionary transfer under Code of Civ. Proc. 397(c) is denied because Respondents fail to meet their burden as the moving party. "The burden rests upon one who seeks a change of venue under [Code of Civ. Proc. 397(c)] to prove that both the convenience of witnesses and the ends of justice will be promoted thereby. This he must do through affidavits" that "set forth the names of the witnesses, the nature of the testimony expected from each, and the reasons why the attendance of each would be inconvenient." (Corfee v. S. Cal. Edison Co. (1962) 202 Cal.App.2d 473, 477.) Respondents fail to provide any such factual support for their motion. Instead, Respondents' argument is that Petitioners "failed to identify any public interest" in deciding the issues in San Francisco. (Resp'ts MPA ISO Mot. 5: 11-12.) "The moving party must stand on the strength of his own showing rather than on any weakness of the opposition." (Corfee, 202 Cal.App.2d at 477.)

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
